2/12
INTRODUCTION

Introduction to Decision Trees

Decision trees are a type of machine learning model used for classification and regression tasks. They learn a hierarchy of if/else questions to make predictions.

1/10
SLIDE 04

Theory and Core Concepts

Decision trees essentially learn a hierarchy of if/else questions, leading to a decision. They can be used for classification and regression tasks. For continuous data, the questions are of the form 'Is feature i larger than value a?'

  • Decision trees learn a hierarchy of if/else questions📄 Page 84
  • Used for classification and regression tasks📄 Page 84
  • Questions for continuous data are of the form 'Is feature i larger than value a?'📄 Page 85
📄 Page 84-85
🔍 Click to enlarge
Figure 2-28. Feature importances computed from a decision tree learned on the Breast — Page 92
2/10
SLIDE 05

Implementation and Examples

The visualization of the tree provides a great in-depth view of how the algorithm makes predictions. A decision tree can be built based on a newly created dataset. The algorithm learns the sequence of if/else questions to make predictions.

  • Visualization of the tree provides insight into predictions📄 Page 91
  • Decision tree can be built based on a newly created dataset📄 Page 98
  • Algorithm learns the sequence of if/else questions📄 Page 85
📄 Page 85, 91, 98
🔍 Click to enlarge
Figure 2-27. Visualization of the decision tree built on the Breast Cancer dataset — Page 90
3/10
SLIDE 06

Parameters and Limitations

Decision trees tend to overfit the training data. Random forests are one way to address this issue. They combine the predictions of multiple trees to reduce overfitting.

  • Decision trees tend to overfit the training data📄 Page 97
  • Random forests combine predictions of multiple trees📄 Page 97
  • Random forests reduce overfitting📄 Page 97
📄 Page 97
🔍 Click to enlarge
Figure 2-32. Comparison of predictions made by a linear model and predictions made — Page 96
4/10
SLIDE 07

Ensembles of Decision Trees

Ensembles of decision trees, such as random forests, combine the predictions of multiple trees to make more accurate predictions. They can handle different types of features, including binary and continuous features.

  • Ensembles combine predictions of multiple trees📄 Page 97
  • Can handle different types of features📄 Page 97
  • Random forests are an example of ensembles of decision trees📄 Page 97
📄 Page 97
🔍 Click to enlarge
Figure 2-53. Decision functions learned with the same parameters but different random — Page 128
5/10
SLIDE 08

Implementation of Ensembles

The algorithm for building an ensemble of decision trees is similar to the algorithm for building a single decision tree. However, the ensemble algorithm combines the predictions of multiple trees to make more accurate predictions.

  • Ensemble algorithm combines predictions of multiple trees📄 Page 98
  • Similar to algorithm for building a single decision tree📄 Page 98
  • Combines predictions to make more accurate predictions📄 Page 98
📄 Page 98
🔍 Click to enlarge
Figure 2-22. A decision tree to distinguish among several animals — Page 85
6/10
SLIDE 09

Parameters and Tradeoffs of Ensembles

The number of trees in an ensemble can be adjusted to trade off between accuracy and computational cost. Increasing the number of trees can improve accuracy but also increases computational cost.

  • Number of trees can be adjusted for tradeoff📄 Page 102
  • Increasing number of trees can improve accuracy📄 Page 102
  • Increasing number of trees also increases computational cost📄 Page 102
📄 Page 102
🔍 Click to enlarge
Figure 2-1. Trade-off of model complexity against training and test accuracy — Page 43
7/10
SLIDE 10

Feature Importances

Decision trees and decision tree-based models provide a feature_importances_ attribute, which can be used to select the most important features for a model.

  • Decision trees provide feature_importances_ attribute📄 Page 252
  • Feature_importances_ can be used for feature selection📄 Page 252
  • Feature_importances_ is based on coefficient or impurity-based intuition📄 Page 252
📄 Page 252
🔍 Click to enlarge
Figure 2-34. Feature importances computed from a random forest that was fit to the — Page 101
8/10
SLIDE 11

Binning, Discretization, Linear Models, and Trees

Binning and discretization are techniques used to transform continuous data into categorical data. Decision trees can be used with binning and discretization to improve model performance.

  • Binning and discretization transform continuous data📄 Page 234
  • Decision trees can be used with binning and discretization📄 Page 234
  • Binning and discretization can improve model performance📄 Page 234
📄 Page 234
🔍 Click to enlarge
Figure 4-1. Comparing linear regression and a decision tree on the wave dataset — Page 235
9/10
✦

Summary

Decision trees are a type of machine learning model used for classification and regression tasks. They can be used alone or in ensembles to improve accuracy and reduce overfitting.

Decision trees
10/10
